stimulus: To address the susceptibility of materials used in deep-sea pipeline coatings to cathodic disbondment (the loss of adhesion between a protective coating and a metallic substrate, which often occurs under prolonged seawater immersion and applied electrical potential), corrosion researchers have developed several types of carbon nanotube (CNT) reinforced epoxy coatings. Testing the long-term performance of several commercially available CNT–epoxy systems, Helena Vargas et al. found that in comparison with two solvent-cast CNT–epoxy coatings, a vacuum-infused CNT–epoxy coating exhibited a smaller maximum disbondment radius, a measure of how far adhesion is lost around a coating defect at constant temperature and applied potential. The finding suggests that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: unlike the two solvent-cast CNT–epoxy coatings, the vacuum-infused coating can entirely prevent disbondment, provided that temperature and applied potential are held constant.
composites based on the two solvent-cast CNT–epoxy coatings likely hold similar potential for reducing the adhesion of coatings exposed to seawater, thereby extending the life of deep-sea pipelines.

deep-sea pipeline coatings containing vacuum-infused CNT–epoxy may retain adhesion around coating defects across a larger area than coatings containing either of the two solvent-cast CNT–epoxy systems.

coatings based on the two solvent-cast CNT–epoxy systems have chemical properties that may improve the long-term integrity of deep-sea pipelines to a greater extent than do coatings based on the vacuum-infused CNT–epoxy system.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. The text reports that the vacuum-infused CNT–epoxy coating exhibited a smaller maximum disbondment radius than the solvent-cast coatings did—meaning adhesion is lost over a smaller area around any defect. The most measured inference is that the vacuum-infused coating retains adhesion across a larger area around defects than the solvent-cast coatings do.

Choice A is incorrect because the measurement concerns how far disbondment spreads, not whether it can be entirely prevented. Choice B is incorrect because reducing adhesion would shorten, not extend, pipeline life; the text actually points in the opposite direction. Choice D is incorrect because the vacuum-infused system performed better, so the solvent-cast systems are not the more promising option.
